questionText: The random variable $Z$ has the standard normal distribution, $Z \sim N(0,1)$. Find $P(Z < 1.2)$, giving your answer to $4$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and what is required
steps.explanation: Here $Z$ is already the standard normal variable, with mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$. We want the probability that $Z$ is less than $1.2$.

steps.description: Note that no standardising is needed
steps.explanation: Because $Z$ is already standardised, we do not have to convert anything; we can read the cumulative probability directly.

steps.description: Write the probability as a value of the CDF
steps.explanation: The probability that $Z$ lies below a value is exactly the standard normal cumulative distribution function $\Phi$ evaluated at that value.

steps.description: Reason about the size of the answer
steps.explanation: Since $1.2$ lies to the right of the mean $0$, more than half of the bell-shaped area lies to its left, so we expect an answer bigger than $0.5$.
Reading the standard normal cumulative distribution at $z = 1.2$ gives $0.8849$ to $4$ significant figures, which is indeed above $0.5$ as predicted.
So about $88.5\%$ of the standard normal distribution lies below $z = 1.2$.
